args.query: site:wellsfargo.com contact customer service wire transfer fraud phone

content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: How To Report Fraud on Personal and Small Business Accounts

  - url: https://www.wellsfargo.com/privacy-security/fraud/report/

  - source: Wells Fargo

  - domain: wellsfargo.com


  ## Content


  This Wells Fargo page explains how personal and small-business customers should
  report fraud or suspicious activity.


  If something does not look right, Wells Fargo tells customers to contact the bank.
  Wells Fargo says it will review accounts, identify unauthorized transactions, and
  help keep accounts secure. Customers traveling or living outside the United States
  may use Wells Fargo international calling options, and Wells Fargo accepts relay
  calls.


  For checking or savings account, debit-card, or ATM-card fraud, Wells Fargo tells
  personal account customers to call 1-800-869-3557. Small-business account customers
  should call 1-800-225-5935. Customers should monitor account activity closely and
  report anything suspicious right away.


  For Bill Pay, Zelle, Direct Pay, online transfers, and digital wires transactions,
  Wells Fargo provides a specific fraud number. If a customer suspects fraud on the
  account, including Wells Fargo Online profile changes, the customer should call
  1-866-867-5568. The page also links to information about bank-imposter scams involving
  Zelle or wires.


  For suspicious emails or text messages, the page distinguishes between cases where
  the customer interacted with the message and cases where the customer did not respond.
  If the customer clicked a suspicious link, opened an attachment, sent a payment,
  shared one-time access codes, or provided personal financial information, Wells
  Fargo says to call 1-866-867-5568. If the customer did not respond, they can forward
  the suspicious email or text message to reportphish@wellsfargo.com.


  The page includes other fraud categories such as elder fraud, identity theft, credit-card
  fraud, and commercial-account fraud. It also states that Wells Fargo will never
  ask over the phone for the customer''s online-banking password.

  '
metadata: {}
title: How To Report Fraud on Personal and Small Business Accounts
url: https://www.wellsfargo.com/privacy-security/fraud/report/

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Digital Wires FAQs

  - url: https://www.wellsfargo.com/help/online-banking/wires-faqs/

  - source: Wells Fargo

  - domain: wellsfargo.com


  ## Content


  Digital Wires FAQs explain how Wells Fargo customers send wire transfers through
  Wells Fargo Online.


  To begin a digital wire, sign on to Wells Fargo Online, go to Transfer & Pay, and
  select Wire Money. Add the recipient, choose the funding account, enter the wire
  amount, review the details, and send the payment. Domestic wires require the recipient
  bank routing number and account number. International wires may require a SWIFT/BIC,
  an IBAN, and country-specific payment details.


  Wells Fargo states that digital wires are subject to eligibility requirements, applicable
  terms and conditions, and fees. Customers must have a valid U.S. mobile number or
  RSA SecurID device to send a wire.


  Cutoff times affect when a wire is sent. If a submitted wire meets the relevant
  cutoff time, Wells Fargo sends it on the same business day; otherwise, the wire
  is sent on the next business day. Domestic wires, international wires, and wires
  to another Wells Fargo account have different cutoff times. Additional review may
  affect when a wire is sent. For security, a wire may go through internal review
  before it is sent, and Wells Fargo may need to contact the customer to verify the
  payment.


  Canceling a wire depends on the wire status. Not all wires can be canceled. A wire
  with a Scheduled status can be canceled from the Confirmation page, Wire details
  page, or Activity section on the Wire Money home page. Scheduled means the wire
  has not started processing yet and can still be canceled.


  Pending means the wire is in process and can no longer be canceled. Completed means
  the wire has been sent successfully. Failed means Wells Fargo was unable to send
  the money and will return the funds to the account. Canceled means the wire was
  canceled and will not be sent. Returned means the wire was not accepted by the recipient
  bank and Wells Fargo will return the funds. Under review means the wire is undergoing
  additional verification for security; while under review, a customer may also have
  the option to cancel by calling Wells Fargo at 877-499-6920.


  Once a wire is processing, Wells Fargo updates the payment status and the customer
  cannot cancel it through the ordinary cancellation flow.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Digital Wires FAQs
url: https://www.wellsfargo.com/help/online-banking/wires-faqs/

This looks like a **suspected recipient-impersonation/payment-instruction scam**, not merely a delayed wire:

- Wire: **domestic, $9,800 USD**
- Sent: **July 15, 2026**
- Transfer ID: **wire_20260715_001**
- The payment instructions were replaced through **email**.
- The intended recipient denied owning the receiving account.
- The wire was **released and accepted by the receiving bank** on July 15, so ordinary cancellation may no longer be available.

### Do this now

1. **Call Wells Fargo immediately using an official number.**  
   Report suspected fraud involving a digital wire and request an urgent **wire recall/recall request, receiving-bank contact, and trace**. Wells Fargo’s fraud page lists **1-866-867-5568** for suspected fraud involving digital wires and related online activity.  
   Official page: [Wells Fargo — How to Report Fraud](https://www.wellsfargo.com/privacy-security/fraud/report/)

2. Provide these details:
   - Transfer ID: `wire_20260715_001`
   - Amount/date: **$9,800 on July 15, 2026**
   - Intended recipient’s name and account details
   - That the recipient **denies ownership**
   - That payment instructions were changed via email
   - Any suspicious email, sender address, headers, attachments, or messages

3. Ask specifically whether Wells Fargo can:
   - Place an immediate **recall request** with the receiving bank
   - Send a **fraud notification/hold request**
   - Provide a case number and written confirmation
   - Tell you whether additional account-security steps are needed

4. **Do not send another payment** and do not rely on the updated email instructions. Contact the recipient using a previously verified phone number or other independent channel.

5. Preserve all evidence, change your Wells Fargo password if the instructions involved your account or email, enable multifactor authentication, and review recent account activity. Do not share your password or one-time codes with anyone claiming to help.

Wells Fargo’s wire FAQ explains that cancellation depends on status and that completed wires have been sent; because this one was already released, act through the **fraud and wire-recall process immediately**, without assuming recovery is guaranteed.

final_answer:
This looks like a **suspected recipient-impersonation/payment-instruction scam**, not merely a delayed wire:

- Wire: **domestic, $9,800 USD**
- Sent: **July 15, 2026**
- Transfer ID: **wire_20260715_001**
- The payment instructions were replaced through **email**.
- The intended recipient denied owning the receiving account.
- The wire was **released and accepted by the receiving bank** on July 15, so ordinary cancellation may no longer be available.

### Do this now

1. **Call Wells Fargo immediately using an official number.**  
   Report suspected fraud involving a digital wire and request an urgent **wire recall/recall request, receiving-bank contact, and trace**. Wells Fargo’s fraud page lists **1-866-867-5568** for suspected fraud involving digital wires and related online activity.  
   Official page: [Wells Fargo — How to Report Fraud](https://www.wellsfargo.com/privacy-security/fraud/report/)

2. Provide these details:
   - Transfer ID: `wire_20260715_001`
   - Amount/date: **$9,800 on July 15, 2026**
   - Intended recipient’s name and account details
   - That the recipient **denies ownership**
   - That payment instructions were changed via email
   - Any suspicious email, sender address, headers, attachments, or messages

3. Ask specifically whether Wells Fargo can:
   - Place an immediate **recall request** with the receiving bank
   - Send a **fraud notification/hold request**
   - Provide a case number and written confirmation
   - Tell you whether additional account-security steps are needed

4. **Do not send another payment** and do not rely on the updated email instructions. Contact the recipient using a previously verified phone number or other independent channel.

5. Preserve all evidence, change your Wells Fargo password if the instructions involved your account or email, enable multifactor authentication, and review recent account activity. Do not share your password or one-time codes with anyone claiming to help.

Wells Fargo’s wire FAQ explains that cancellation depends on status and that completed wires have been sent; because this one was already released, act through the **fraud and wire-recall process immediately**, without assuming recovery is guaranteed.
